title: OLAP Lakehouse: Databricks
description: Databricks lakehouse pattern using Delta Lake tables, Unity Catalog, and Databricks SQL / BI integrations as the primary analytical platform. Best when the team explicitly wants Databricks as the managed lakehouse provider and values a unified data engineering, data science, and SQL-serving environment over cloud-native point services.

cost.costNotes: Databricks combines compute, catalog, and SQL-serving costs. It is typically more expensive than the cheapest cloud-native lakehouse defaults, but offers a more integrated managed lakehouse experience.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Min ($300): development SQL warehouse and modest jobs footprint. Max ($15,000): multiple SQL warehouses, pipelines, and larger compute for production workloads. Real spend varies heavily by workload shape and warehouse sizing.
provenance.adoptionCost: Adoption cost covers workspace setup, Unity Catalog, Delta design, ingestion/job pipelines, and SQL-serving integration. Estimate 18-22 developer days at ~$160/day = $3,500.
provenance.licenseCost: No traditional license fee beyond Databricks consumption pricing.
provenance.source: LLM analysis informed by Databricks documentation (as of 13-Apr-2026)

provides.reasoning: Databricks SQL provides managed analytical query execution over Delta Lake data.
Databricks SQL and external BI integrations support dashboards and reporting over the lakehouse.
Databricks supports batch and pipeline-oriented data engineering workloads in the same platform.
Databricks lakehouse data remains in cloud object storage under a managed lakehouse control plane.
Unity Catalog provides centralized metadata, governance, and access control.
Delta Lake is the default open table format for Databricks lakehouse workloads.

requires.reasoning: Databricks lakehouse value comes from actively managed ingestion, transformation, and table maintenance.
Databricks still depends on cloud IAM and object-storage access controls.

availability.reason: Cross-cloud SaaS lakehouse pattern when Databricks is explicitly chosen

supports_nfr.reason: Databricks SQL warehouses can support sub-second interactive analytics, but the default lakehouse posture is not framed as sub-200ms serving.
Managed Databricks services can support up to four nines depending on service and cloud posture.
Managed service recovery is usually faster than self-hosted but still bounded by workspace and warehouse restart behavior.
RPO is tied to ingestion and Delta commit/update cadence.
Databricks SQL can support moderate-to-high concurrent analytical usage, but the default posture does not assume warehouse fleets sized for the very highest BI concurrency.
The default lakehouse posture assumes batch and streaming table maintenance rather than high-frequency point-write semantics.
Managed Databricks jobs and SQL-serving startup are faster than self-hosted stacks but still have non-zero orchestration and cluster warm-up overhead.
Tail job starts can be affected by warehouse warm-up, cluster spin-up, or queueing in the managed Databricks control plane.
The default Databricks posture can handle more job churn than most cloud-native lakehouse defaults, but this family still does not assume extreme short-job frequency.
Databricks supports enterprise identity patterns and does not constrain application auth choices.
Managed service logging and catalog governance support auditability.
PII can be governed through Unity Catalog and cloud storage controls.
Compatibility depends on governance and data-handling controls rather than platform choice alone.
HIPAA compatibility depends on workspace, cloud, encryption, and operational controls.
CCPA compatibility depends on lineage and deletion workflows across the lakehouse.
SOX compatibility depends on catalog governance, auditability, and operational control.
Durability depends on the underlying cloud object storage and workspace configuration.

supports_constraints.reason: This variant activates only when Databricks is explicitly allowed as the lakehouse provider.
Databricks is available across the major public clouds.
Databricks lakehouse access is language-agnostic across SQL, jobs, and APIs.
Databricks lakehouse can serve the analytical plane behind web, mobile, desktop, and API products while also supporting worker, batch, and data-pipeline execution.
Primary gate: this variant is for OLAP analytical workloads.

catalog.description: Unity Catalog is the default metadata and governance plane for Databricks lakehouse workloads.

table_format.description: Delta Lake is the default Databricks table format. Iceberg interoperability is an alternative when open-format portability is a stronger requirement.

sql_serving.description: Serverless SQL warehouse is the default because it best matches the advertised default interactive serving posture.

bi_layer.description: Databricks SQL is the default reporting surface; external BI tools are valid when the organization standardizes elsewhere.

ingestion_mode.description: Auto Loader plus Delta Live Tables is the default managed ingestion posture.
